**SmartSmile: A Web-Based and Mobile Accessible AI System for Preventive Oral Health Screening Using Smartphone Images**

**Date: 27th January 2026**

**List of Acronyms/Abbreviations**   
AI \- Artificial Intelligence  
BSc. \- Bachelor of Science  
CNN \- Convolutional Neural Network  
CNNs \- Convolutional Neural Networks  
ERD \- Entity Relationship Diagram  
F1-score / F1-score(s) \- Harmonic mean of precision and recall  
GBD \- Global Burden of Disease  
IHME \- Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation  
mHealth \- Mobile Health  
ML \- Machine Learning  
RGB \- Red, Green, Blue (image color model)  
SUS \- System Usability Scale  
UML \- Unified Modeling Language  
WHO \- World Health Organization  
YOLO \- You Only Look Once  
YOLOv3 \- You Only Look Once version 3  
YOLOv5 / YOLOv5s \- You Only Look Once version 5 / small variant  
R-CNN \- Region-based Convolutional Neural Network  
Faster R-CNN \- Faster Region-based Convolutional Neural Network  
U-net \-  U-shaped Convolutional Neural Network  
MobileNet / MobileNet-v3  Lightweight Convolutional Neural Network for mobile devices

# **CHAPTER ONE: INTRODUCTION**  {#chapter-one:-introduction}

 **1.1 Introduction and Background**

Oral diseases are among the most intractable and prevalent issues in the global health of billions of people and significantly contribute to morbidity in the world. The World Health Organization estimates that over a quarter of the world's population has untreated dental caries, and approximately 1 billion people worldwide have severe periodontitis (World Health Organization, 2024). As of 2021, approximately 3.69 billion individuals were projected to experience at least one form of oral disorder, including untreated caries and gum disease, which shows that the burden of oral health problems continues to be high in various locations and among different age groups (GBD Oral Conditions Report, 2025). These statistics indicate the chronicity of oral diseases as well as a high amount of unmet preventive and early-care needs.

Over the last thirty years, the rate of dental caries and periodontal disease in the world has not decreased significantly, despite the development of dental technologies and health education. In a recent study by the Global Burden of Disease Study 2021, it was established that although the prevalence of some forms of caries has stalled, periodontal disease is very prevalent, with the greatest prevalence in middle-aged and older adults, with a substantial regional disparity, in particular in low and middle income countries(Huang, Kang, and Bi, 2025). In most low resource settings, accessibility to dental practitioners, high treatment prices, and poor awareness are some of the barriers that lead to delayed treatment and progression of the disease. Such disparities in the system indicate the necessity of new methods that will enhance early diagnosis in non-hospital settings.

Although oral prevalence is quite high, the existing oral health care systems are mostly reactive and not preventive. A majority of people seek dental treatment once the pain or other symptoms have already been experienced, which in most instances signifies the occurrence of the disease in its advanced stages. The complexity of treatment could be immensely decreased by early diagnosis and preventive advice, but screening resources are unavailable. In addition, socioeconomic factors like income disparity and inadequate dental workforce density have also been depicted to enhance oral health disparities, especially in underserved individuals (Liang et al., 2025). Such inequalities stress the need to come up with screening tools that are affordable yet simple to apply in the community.

Over the last several years, there has been a revolution in the field of image analysis through machine learning and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in particular, which have revolutionized the medical sector of analysis. Deep learning models have been shown to perform well in identifying dental features and pathologies in a clinical image, such as radiographs and intraoral photographs. As an example, dental decay diagnosis in annotated datasets on photographic images has been reported to have F1-scores between approximately 78% and over 90% with deep learning systems, which indicates the potential of the methods in dental diagnostics (systematic review on AI dental image detection, 2025). A lot of this work has however been done on images taken under controlled clinical environments with specific equipment as opposed to consumer level images.

Mobile health (mHealth) mobile technologies present an opportunity to address and close this disparity in clinical knowledge and real people. The proliferation of smartphones that have high-resolution cameras and enormous processing power is a potential that can be used to democratize access to early health screening devices. Little initial research has considered the viability of smartphone-based detection of dental conditions, but these systems are still under development and are frequently constrained in either their scopes or datasets or real-time operation. As an example, AI applications to images taken with smartphones have demonstrated mixed results, mainly because of the issue of image quality, light exposure, and the disparity between the real-life scenarios.

As such, there is a strong desire to have an available, machine learning based solution to oral health screening that is capable of processing images taken with a smartphone in real-time and offer valuable, preventative feedback. This kind of a system would not aim to substitute clinical diagnosis but would aim at increasing early awareness, encouraging relevant professional consultation, and empowering individuals with practical knowledge. With the emphasis on user-centered approach design and the consumer-grade images, this study will address one of the key gaps in preventive oral health, mainly in the context of areas with a low access to traditional dental care.

 **1.2 Problem statement**

The number of oral diseases like dental caries and periodontal inflammation are still the most widespread types of health problems on a global scale, impacting billions of individuals and resulting in pain, tooth loss, and poor quality of life in case they are not caught in time (Global Burden of Disease, 2025). In spite of the fact that clinical dental examination and radiographic examination can accurately identify oral health problems, they involve personnel training and specialized equipment, which is not always available in low-resource environments (Liang et al., 2025). Consequently, due to this, most people do not seek treatment at early stages before things get worse costing more to treat and preventable illnesses.

Most recent attempts have used machine learning to aid in dental image recognition. Models of caries detection with the help of systems such as DeepDent and AI have demonstrated encouraging results in clinical practices by detecting dental caries with a radiograph and intraoral images (Thanh, Van Toan, Vo Truong Nhu, and Nguyen, 2022). These methods, however, are based on regulated imaging conditions and data obtained using professional equipment so they can only be useful in dental clinics and research settings. They are not in favor of real-time analysis based on images captured by common people in normal settings.

Mobile health applications and smartphone accessible web systems have been developed too that can involve users in oral health education and risk monitoring. As an example, the BrushSync and OralCare Tracker applications can remind a user about the need to brush and give some general recommendations, but they do not use machine learning to identify the real state of the mouth based on the photo (Nzabonimana et al., 2024). Although this kind of application enhances awareness, it does not provide individual feedback on the detectable evidence of dental problems in the images captured by the user.

There is a new interest in using artificial intelligence on dental photographs acquired through smartphones in research activities. Early researchers have investigated automated detection of plaque and estimation of gingivism-related inflammation with convolutional neural networks, with moderate results in the presence of controlled conditions (Ahmed and Lee, 2024). But the models that are currently available are either trained on small, curated datasets or tested in the lab, and fail to be effective when exposed to real-world problems such as variable lighting, unequal image quality, and different oral anatomies.

Regardless of these developments, however, no generally accessible technology is available that can allow real-time and preventive dental health screening based on smartphone images and machine learning that is consumer grade and easy to use. The nearest options refer to the support of clinical diagnosis or the general reminders of oral health, which creates a vacuum in the scope of preventive aids that might be applied at any time and place and give immediate feedback to the user on whether they are showing visible symptoms of the dental diseases. Such a gap leads to the further disregard of the initial oral health problems and the loss of the possibility to visit a professional organization in time.

Therefore, there is a need for a machine learning-based system that can analyze smartphone images taken by ordinary users, detect visible indicators of common oral health problems such as caries, plaque buildup, and gingival redness, and provide preventive guidance to support early awareness and care-seeking behavior.

**1.3 Project’s main objective**

The main objective of this project is to design and develop a machine learning-based system capable of analyzing dental images captured using smartphone cameras in order to support early and preventive oral health screening. The system aims to use computer vision techniques to identify visible indicators of common oral health conditions, such as dental caries, plaque accumulation, and gingival inflammation, and to provide users with real-time, easy to understand feedback that encourages timely professional consultation.

**1.3.1 List of the specific objectives**  
 

1. Review existing literature, technologies, and digital tools related to oral health screening, machine learning applications in dentistry, and smartphone-based medical image analysis in order to understand current approaches, identify limitations, and clearly define the research gap addressed by this project.  
2. To design and develop a machine learning-based system that can analyze dental images captured using smartphone cameras and identify visible indicators of common oral health conditions, such as dental caries, plaque buildup, and gingival inflammation.  
3. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed system by collecting and analyzing measurable outcomes related to both technical performance and preventive impact. This includes assessing the accuracy and reliability of the image analysis model, as well as examining whether the system improves early awareness of oral health issues

**1.4 Research questions**  
 

1. What are the existing approaches and limitations in current oral health screening tools and machine learning-based dental image analysis systems, particularly those intended for public or non-clinical use?

2. How effectively can a machine learning model analyze smartphone-captured dental images to identify visible indicators of common oral health conditions in real-time, non-clinical settings?

3. To what extent does the proposed system demonstrate measurable effectiveness in supporting preventive oral health screening, in terms of model performance and its potential to improve early awareness and decision-making related to professional dental care?

**1.5 Project scope**  
This research will focus on the design, development, and pilot evaluation of a machine learning-based preventive oral health screening system that analyzes dental images captured using smartphone cameras. The project is defined to ensure meaningful results within academic time, while clearly distinguishing preventive screening from clinical diagnosis.

### **1.5.1 Geographic and Population Scope** {#1.5.1-geographic-and-population-scope}

The geographic scope of this study targets populations in low and middle income contexts, with particular relevance to urban areas in Rwanda, with broader relevance to similar low and middle income settings in Sub-Saharan Africa where smartphone usage is high but access to regular dental care remains limited. The pilot evaluation will involve participants recruited digitally across different regions through online platforms, university networks, and community based channels rather than country-wide deployment. This approach reflects evidence showing increasing smartphone penetration in developing regions, where ownership among young adults and adults exceeds 80% in many urban settings (International Telecommunication Union, 2023).

The target group will include the adolescents and adults aged 16 years and older that possess a smartphone with a working camera and have basic access to the internet. Pilot testing would include about 50-100 interviewees, which is mentioned as appropriate to use in the initial assessment of the digital health tools. Medical training will not be necessary as the system is aimed at a general group of users, taking pictures outside the clinical setting. This population target corresponds to the international guidelines that highlight the importance of oral health prevention at the community level and using technologies (World Health Organization, 2023).

### **1.5.2 Temporal Scope** {#1.5.2-temporal-scope}

The project will be conducted within a defined academic research period of approximately 12 weeks. This timeframe will include initial requirements analysis and dataset preparation, followed by model development, system integration, and pilot evaluation. The system evaluation will focus on short-term outcomes, such as model performance, usability, and preventive feedback relevance, rather than long-term clinical or behavioral outcomes.

The temporal scope is consistent with recent pilot studies in mobile-accessible web system and AI-based screening tools, which emphasize short term feasibility and performance evaluation before larger longitudinal studies (Ahmed et al., 2024; Solea & Berg, 2022).

### **1.5.3 Technological Scope** {#1.5.3-technological-scope}

The proposed project will be launched as a mobile-accessible web system with a machine learning-based model of dental image analysis. The system will be based on convolutional neural networks that will be trained on annotated datasets of dental images to recognize visible signs of tooth discoloration, the existence of plaque, and redness of the gingiva. The consumer-grade smartphone cameras will be used to take pictures in natural conditions i.e. in the open air and not in a controlled clinical place.

Radiographic imaging, specific dental equipment, and external diagnostic tools will not be included in the system because they demand professional skills and facilities. It is also supposed to have a purposely restricted technological design to promote accessibility, cost-efficiency, and scalability, in line with the suggestions of AI-assisted preventive health tools in resource-constrained environments (European Commission, 2023).

### **1.5.4 Methodological Scope** {#1.5.4-methodological-scope}

This research adopts a design and evaluate approach, combining system development with preliminary effectiveness assessment. Quantitative evaluation will include technical metrics such as model accuracy, precision, and recall in identifying visible oral health indicators from smartphone images, as well as system response time and reliability. In addition, user centered metrics such as perceived usefulness and clarity of feedback will be collected through structured questionnaires.

The feedback will be collected qualitatively by using open-ended survey responses to receive the experiences of the users and the perceived limitations of the system. The test is meant to give preliminary accountability of viability and preventive worth as compared to conclusive clinical validation. This strategy is in line with previous designs in the research of early-stage digital health and AI screening, where preliminary studies are about usability, technical capabilities, and relevance, followed by large-scale clinical trials (Park et al., 2020).

**1.6 Significance and Justification**

The successful adoption of the SmartSmile system can generate significant health, economic and technological advantages of supporting an early and preventive oral health screening with smartphone photos. Oral diseases include some of the most costly non-communicable diseases to prevent and treat once reaching advanced stages and restorative and surgical dental services are several times more expensive than preventive care (World Health Organization, 2024). The prevalence of visible oral health issues, including accumulation of plaque, early caries, and gingivitis can be greatly prevented by prompt detection of oral health issues at an early stage in case the users are motivated to visit a professional care. Research indicates that prevention dental procedures may lower the expenses of oral health treatment over time by as much as 30-40 percent, specifically in the groups with uneven distribution of dental services (Peres et al., 2023). SmartSmile can ensure less oral health seekers receive delayed care by offering them real-time feedback through their phones, which will allow them to make timely oral healthcare choices.

Dental consultation is a huge obstacle cost-wise in most of the low and middle income environments, and can make up a significant share of monthly household health spending. It has been shown that, in such situations, over 60 percent of the population does not visit the dentist until the pain is so intense (Liang et al., 2025). An early awareness tool that is a preventive screening tool but does not involve booking of immediate clinic appointments can minimize the unnecessary delays and costs and refer the users to the right professional care. Thus, this project can be justified by the fact that it has the potential to reduce the societal and economic cost of the untreated oral disease as it focuses on reactive treatment instead of early awareness and prevention of oral disease.

### **1.6.1 Academic and Research Significance** {#1.6.1-academic-and-research-significance}

Academically, the study will add to the emerging area of artificial intelligence-based preventive medicine through the empirical findings on the application of smartphone-based dental image analysis outside the clinical setting. Although it has been shown that machine learning models are practical in the analysis of the dental images previously, the majority of the current research is conducted with radiographs, or in-offer intraoral photographs (Estai et al., 2022; Ahmed and Lee, 2024). Limited documented studies have been made on real-time preventive screening on consumer-grade smartphone images, especially in non-clinical and resource constrained settings.

### **1.6.2 Technological Significance** {#1.6.2-technological-significance}

Technologically, this project demonstrates how machine learning and mobile-accessible web system computing can be integrated to create an accessible and scalable preventive health tool. The SmartSmile system explores the practical application of convolutional neural networks for analyzing non-clinical dental images captured using smartphone cameras, addressing challenges such as inconsistent lighting, image quality variation, and user-controlled image capture. These challenges are frequently cited as limitations in deploying AI-based health tools in real-world settings (European Commission, 2023).

### **1.6.3 Social and Public Health Significance** {#1.6.3-social-and-public-health-significance}

This project has a social importance because it can be used to enhance oral health awareness and preventive behavior by the general population. The oral health behaviors and care-seeking behaviors are usually determined at early age and have a significant impact on the health outcomes in the future (Zhu et al., 2024). Offering feedback and proactive advice immediately, SmartSmile can prompt the user to take oral health into account prior to the emergence of pain or other severe symptoms and also foster the urge towards self-awareness and, consequently, make informed choices instead of being passively dependent on clinical visits.

**1.7 Research Budget**

# **CHAPTER TWO: LITERATURE REVIEW**  {#chapter-two:-literature-review}

**2.1 Introduction**   
For this project, I focused on software-related literature addressing machine learning for dental image analysis, smartphone based health applications, and real-time preventive screening systems. I conducted a systematic review, searching indexed platforms such as IEEE Xplore, PubMed, ScienceDirect, SpringerLink, ACM Digital Library, and Google Scholar, using keywords like “deep learning dental images,” “mobile-accessible oral health system,” and “machine learning dental screening.” I filtered papers that contained solid software solutions in comparison with the oral health in general review and reviewed approximately 20-25 papers that were relevant. Park et al. (2022) designed a deep learning system based on convolutional neural networks to divide intraoral photographs to detect caries and demonstrated that it was possible to use software based dental screening. Cai et al. (2024) suggested an oral image segmentation model that is highly accurate in detecting calculus, gingivitis, and caries, whereas Garg et al. (2023) developed lightweight neural networks that could be used on smartphones to detect oral diseases in real-time. Other studies note the use of YOLO-based mobile applications to detect dental caries with a high level of accuracy that is comparable to junior dentists (Thanh, Van Toan, Vo Truong Nhu, and Nguyen, 2022), and systematic reviews reveal that convolutional neural networks offer high diagnostic accuracy when incorporated into user-centered software packages (Ahmed and Lee, 2024). On the whole, these articles demonstrate that clinical AI applications are available, but a significant gap appears in the consumer grade, smartphone-based preventive screening that SmartSmile will fulfill with the help of mobile-accessible web system friendly ML models, real-time feedback, and user friendly design. 

**2.2 Historical Background of the Research Topic** 

Oral health management has been developing tremendously in the past twenty years and has shifted the trend of the traditional check up with a dentist to placing the digital technologies and machine learning approach to the early and preventive care. In the past oral health care was primarily based on clinical meetings and professional imaging which included radiographs, and this restricted access to people in low resource environments (World Health Organization, 2024). The initial efforts to assist with preventative oral health using software were mobile health applications that aimed at general oral health care guidance, brushing reminders, and dental disease education (Nzabonimana et al., 2024). Although these applications enhanced awareness, they did not analyse the real time condition of the oral state of the user and failed to make use of AI to offer personalised feedback to the user.

As the machine learning and image processing developed, scientists started creating programs that can study dental images and determine the condition of dental caries, plaque formation, and gingivitis. Initial models, based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs) showed excellent results on professional intraoral photos in clinical settings (Park et al., 2022; Cai et al., 2024). These systems were however restricted to controlled environments and needed high-quality images, and thus not suitable to the ordinary user using smartphones. The more recent software solutions have considered smartphone-based detection with deep learning models such as YOLO and MobileNet that can run on mobile devices efficiently to process RGB images and provide real-time preventative screening (Thanh, Van Toan, Vo Truong Nhu, and Nguyen, 2022). With these developments, there is still a gap in the area of making preventive resources readily available, easy to use and work without the clinics and with consumer-grade pictures they provide immediate feedback to users.

This historical pattern indicates a distinct direction the oral health software moved out of the general education and towards applications based on AI diagnostic features, and even most of the existing applications are structured to be used by a clinic. Mobile computing, combined with deep learning, currently allows implementing preventive oral health platforms, such as SmartSmile, enabling individuals to monitor their own oral health, predict the earliest signs of oral issues, and request the timely professional assistance, even in high-resource or daily contexts (Ahmed and Lee, 2024).

**2.3 Overview of Existing System**   
This part of the survey informs about the existing software applications in the field of dental image analysis and preventive oral health screening. The idea is to demonstrate that even though there is software in place that partially solves the dental care issues, the majority of the systems fail to resolve the issue of the implementation of real time, accessible and preventive screening with the help of smartphone pictures. These systems assist us to realize our strengths and weaknesses, where SmartSmile belongs to the bigger technological environment.

### **2.3.1 AI-Based Mobile-accessible Web System Dental Screening Systems** {#2.3.1-ai-based-mobile-accessible-web-system-dental-screening-systems}

A YOLOv5-based AI smartphone application that is created to identify dental caries in the intraoral image is one of the most developed systems currently available. In a study conducted by Adnan et al. a YOLOv5s model was trained on thousands of annotated intraoral images and was deployed to a smartphone application. Compared to junior dentists, the app demonstrated a high level of performance, a precision of 90.7 percent, a sensitivity of 85.6 percent, and an F1 score of 88.0 percent to detect dental decay (Adnan et al., 2025). This study demonstrates that deep learning would be useful in detecting caries in a mobile setting but it was not tested in uncontrolled natural settings.

Other studies have evaluated YOLOv3 and Faster R-CNN models on smartphones that carry dental images. These studies have discovered that convolutional neural networks are capable of identifying cavitated lesions with a reasonable degree of sensitivity and specificity, but that their performance degrades on much smaller early lesions (Krothapalli & Cherukumalli Kapalavayi, 2025). That implies that deep learning has potential, but all real-world pictures (light, angles, quality) are a significant issue to existing software.

### **2.3.3 Usability‑Focused Oral Health Apps** {#2.3.3-usability‑focused-oral-health-apps}

There are a number of commercial and research applications that deal with user perceptions and self monitoring opposed to automatic diagnostics. As an illustration, an AI-driven application was tested, named TestMyTeeth, which the end-user found useful, but it was unable to obtain clear intraoral images and interface navigation on regular basis (Getachew et al., 2025). These usability issues were not limited to a particular age or education level, and it demonstrates that software usability is still a limitation to successful usage.

Caries risk self assessment and preventive education mHealth app, e.g., CarieCheck, has been created, with the high usability and perceived quality scores (CarieCheck research, 2025). These apps however aid in education and in risk awareness but still fail to incorporate real time machine learning based identification of visible signs on the user photos and therefore are not yet useful as actual screening tools.

### **2.3.4 Prototype and Research Tools** {#2.3.4-prototype-and-research-tools}

Outside commercial sysstem, research prototypes focus on image analysis with interaction with the user. As an example, OralCam is a mobile system (deep learning based) which enables individuals to self-examine various oral conditions with the help of smartphone images and extra user centered context information. OralCam demonstrated good detection sensitivity in laboratory testing, but as a research prototype is not easily accessible as a general use device, and the implementation needs additional evolution to allow its general use by consumers (Liang et al., 2020).

Likewise, machine learning studies such as that of Garg et al. (2023) have created lightweight neural networks that optimize on mobile phones and can detect calculus and other characteristics based on RGB oral images, with promise of near real time analysis, but not yet implemented in apps that are readily available.

**2.4 Review of Related Work**  

Recent research on AI-based dental screening and preventive oral health software has grown, mainly due to the need for accessible, early detection tools. Many studies have explored using machine learning and mobile health technologies to detect dental caries, plaque, gingivitis, and other oral conditions from images captured either in clinical or home settings.

Much attention has been paid to deep learning in dental image analysis. The AI system presented by Adnan et al. (2025) was based on the algorithm known as YOLOv5 and was trained on intraoral photographs to identify dental caries. The system had an F1-score of 88, which demonstrates that convolutional neural networks are capable of detecting carious lesions. The experiment was however done under controlled conditions using professionally captured images which can be a hindrance to the performance of consumer grade photographs. Likewise, Krothapalli and Cherukumalli Kapalavayi (2025) have reviewed AI models such as Faster R-CNN and MobileNet based systems, and have concluded that although AI can be effectively able to detect dental decay with high accuracy, in reality, it may be affected by variations in light, image quality, and user technique and fail to perform as expected.

The other researches were on smartphone-accessible preventive tools. AICaries is a mobile system that was tested by Al-Jallad et al. (2022). The system can guide parents to detect early childhood caries using phone cameras. The research indicated a high level of usability and user satisfaction, which suggested that smartphones have a prospective in preventing oral health. However, the application was also restricted to young children and image quality posed a problem with regard to accuracy of diagnoses. On the same note, TestMyTeeth, which was tested by Al-Zubaidy et al. (2025), gave users an opportunity to monitor dental plaque via AI by themselves, but many of them could not take a clear intraoral picture, and usability scores were not high, which means that it would be difficult to make the system entirely user-friendly.

Oral health screening potential is also mentioned in several research prototypes with AI driving. Liang et al. (2020) created OralCam, an application that uses AI to analyze the pictures of a smartphone and integrate them with the data provided by the users to determine the state of oral health. Though OralCam was encouraging in controlled tests, it is yet to be implemented on a large scale. Garg et al. (2023) came up with lightweight neural networks that are mobile device optimized in detecting calculus and gingiva inflammation. Such models have been able to offer near real-time analysis, which is nevertheless experimental and is not widely accessible to ordinary users.

These researches indicate that AI and mobile technologies play a crucial role in the oral health screening process, and there is a possibility of image-based identification and greater access. Nonetheless, such obstacles like reliance on high-quality images, limited population coverage, usability issues, and decreased accuracy in uncontrolled settings also represent common challenges of the research.

**2.3.1 Summary of Reviewed Literature** 

The literature review shows that artificial intelligence and mobile accessible health technologies have developed greatly in the field of preventive oral health. Research by Krothapalli and Cherukumalli Kapalavayi (2025) indicates that such deep learning models as YOLOv5 and Faster R-CNN can diagnose dental conditions with high precision when using clinical or controlled images. Mobile applications, such as AICaries (Al-Jallad et al., 2022\) and TestMyTeeth (Al-Zubaidy et al., 2025), demonstrate that mobile applications can enhance access to preventive screening and increase oral health awareness among the users. Other options, including OralCam (Liang et al., 2020\) and lightweight mobile neural network prototypes (Garg et al., 2023\) also emphasize that oral health monitoring can be offered with the help of real-time image analysis or near real-time.

In spite of these developments, there are still a number of severe limitations. Majority of the systems depend on quality, professionally taken pictures and thus less accurate when they are employed with standard smartphones. There are numerous apps that are specific to certain groups of people like children and that are not as generalizable to adults in various situations. The common usability problems include the inability to obtain clear intraoral images, light management, or the appropriate angle to capture an image referred to AI detection. In addition, the majority of solutions were tested in controlled or laboratory environment, which restricts the evidence of performance in the real-world environment. Not many applications offer preventive feedback with a customized and actionable feedback to the user, urging them to take the necessary measures or visit the specialists. Lastly, the available tools do not combine various elements of the preventive oral health, including education, real-time screening, and advice on secondary care, into one platform available to users.

These loopholes show that though AI and mobile accessible health applications have been able to show the viability of image-based detection and preventive awareness, there is no existing solution that can handle the requirement of a trustworthy and conveniently applicable real-time preventive oral health check system to general users with consumer-grade smartphones. The lack of research that can produce a system, which integrates proper AI recognition, assisted by images, user-friendly design, and customized preventive feedback, is evident. This gap needs to be bridged to offer accessible oral health solutions in normal settings especially to those with little access to professional dental care. This brings out the need to conduct additional research to develop and test a comprehensive system that can overcome the technical, usability and accessibility constraints of the currently available solutions.

**2.5 Strengths and Weakness of the Existing System(s)**   
Existing software solutions in the field of oral health screening and dental image analysis have shown promising advances, especially with the use of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML). Some systems demonstrate strengths in integrating modern computational techniques and improving accessibility to preventive oral care information. However, many of these software systems still face important limitations that prevent them from fully solving the problem of accessible, accurate, real‑time, preventive oral health screening for everyday smartphone users.

An obvious advantage of existing solutions is the use of AI and deep learning models to identify dental conditions in images. Research has revealed that deep-learning based AI systems are capable of good performance in the detection of dental caries and other oral health related indicators using photographic data and clinical images as input. A systematic review of deep learning-based dental caries detection models stated that deep learning has high diagnostic accuracy, F1-scores and detection rates comparable to the traditional diagnostic method (Vach et al., 2024; Shams et al., 2024). Similarly, AI systems built on MobileNet \-v3 and U \-net structures have demonstrated general high-accuracy in detecting caries when used in a clinical setting (Shams et al., 2024). These findings indicate that AI-oriented tools can be used in practice to aid oral diseases detection, and be transferred to mobile applications.

There is also the possibility of accessibility through smart phones, which is another strength. An increasing number of applications, including TestMyTeeth and AICaries, are available to enable users to take pictures of their mouths at home and be provided with the feedback on the results by automated algorithms. To give an example, TestMyTeeth enables users to track their dental plaque through the application of AI, which enables people to interact with their oral health out of the clinic (Al‑Zubaidy et al., 2025). AICaries has been found to be a measure of screening early childhood caries, and parents who used the app claimed that the app is helpful in detecting and educating about risk factors at home (Al‑Jallad et al., 2022). These mobile prehistoric solutions demonstrate that mobile accessible platforms would be able to deliver preventive oral health devices to the daily lives of users.

In spite of these advantages, there are still apparent weaknesses in the current software systems that restrain their utility. One of them is usability problems and difficulties in capturing images. Surveys containing an assessment of TestMyTeeth results revealed that users had difficulties when making clear intra-oral images, particularly of difficult to access posterior teeth, and a significant number of them needed technical assistance to operate the application correctly (Al‑Zubaidy et al., 2025). The mean System Usability Scale (SUS) value of 56.2 showed that it was not good, but only marginally acceptable, which is way below the industry average of good usability (Al‑Zubaidy et al., 2025). This indicates that bad user experience and inability to take good images can diminish the trustworthiness of any type of software screening tool.

The second weakness is that the actual usage and testing of AI models in real-life scenarios have little actual performance and testing, particularly when it is not a controlled clinical environment. Systematic reviews demonstrate that AI models tend to be effective when applied to curated data containing high-quality images, and their performance significantly decreases when applied to images captured by an average user in uncontrolled conditions and settings, including smartphone photos (Shams et al., 2024; Vach et al., 2024). The sensitivity and specificity metrics also do not have standard values across the studies, which points to the fact that a great number of AI systems cannot be used in reality to prevent instead of treat diseases without professional supervision (Shams et al., 2024).

The other weakness has got to do with generalizability and validation. Lots of AI models applied to dental screening have not been extensively validated outside in various population groups, types of devices, and imaging parameters. Devoid of such validation, models can be biased to the data used to train them and not be effective on any user whose demographic or environmental factors do not match the training data particularly in low-resource or underrepresented groups (Vach et al., 2024).

Moreover, most of the available oral health systems are more educational, remedial, or self-monitoring instead of actual diagnostic screening with rich image analysis. MH oral health Scoping reviews indicate that although numerous apps facilitate the use of hygiene routines, few of them include advanced image-based AI diagnostics or remote clinical studies, or put differently, they do not detect even the initial signs of illness on the basis of photos (Väyrynen et al., 2023; Al-Zubaidy et al., 2025).

To summarize, the existing software to perform oral health screening is not only significant empirical progress in terms of AI integration and mobile accessibility, but also lacks a usability level, real-world functionality, external validation, and advanced diagnostic features. Such constraints imply that the current systems are frequently unable to deliver precise, personalized, real-time feedback to the common individuals based on smartphone-taken photos. SmartSmile will close these gaps by aiding usability, increasing the strength of AI analysis of consumer level photos, and providing actionable preventative advice to users regardless of their diverse real-world settings.

**2.6 General comment and Conclusion**  
The findings of this literature review are a good support to the present research study. It goes without saying that mobile accessible health technologies and artificial intelligence have been already used successfully in the context of preventive oral health screening (Krothapalli & Cherukumalli Kapalavayi, 2025), and the analysis of dental images has been established as a promising area of research with extensive descriptions of future uses in caries, plaque, and other prevalent conditions (Al-Zubaidy et al., 2022; Al-Jallad et al., 2025). This creates an obvious reason to create such a system and proves that it is possible to combine the AI-driven analysis with the accessibility of the smartphone. Nevertheless, there are crucial gaps on the intersection of population specific needs and real-life situations, such as difficulties with consumer-level image capture, the variety of users, usability issues, and missing personalized preventive feedback (Liang et al., 2020; Garg et al., 2023). Further future measures should thus aim at overcoming these limitations by coming up with a system that will facilitate proper real-time screening, user friendly instructions towards the capture of clear intraoral images and practical recommendations that will lead to early referral to the professional. These findings are used to develop specific technical architecture, model development and functionality strategies that have been detailed in the following chapter, using the system analysis and design.

# **CHAPTER THREE: SYSTEM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN**  {#chapter-three:-system-analysis-and-design}

                           
**3.1 Introduction** 

This chapter provides the system analysis and system design strategies that were used in the creation of the SmartSmile preventive oral health screening system. The chapter describes the methodology intended to comprehend user requirements, establish the specifications of the system, and come up with a solution that incorporates machine learning with mobile accessible technology to analyse dental images in real-time. The design oriented research methodology is adopted which involves literature based analysis and application of practical system development to serve the gap in the literature review on available oral health screening tools.

The research is developed in a systematic system development process comprising: requirements analysis, system modeling and iterative design. Principles of user-centered design are used in such a way that the system can be useful and suitable to the non-clinical user who takes the dental pictures with the smartphone. Regarding research, the methodology incorporates both technical and evaluative elements, which allow assessing the functionality of the system, model performance, and relevance as preventive. The design choices in this chapter are designed to be made so that the proposed system is feasible technically, ethically responsible and concurs with the preventive goals of the research.

**3.2 System Requirements Analysis**

Analysis of the system requirements is an important step in the process of developing the SmartSmile system because it outlines what the system is supposed to perform and what conditions the system must perform. This stage deals with the translation of the problem that was established in the previous chapters into tangible system requirements that will inform the design and implementation effort. The literature on preventive oral health screening, the available digital health tools, and practical limitations related to using smartphones to analyze pictures in non-clinical settings will help inform the analysis.

The requirements were determined based on literature review, analysis of the similar oral health and mobile health systems, as well as the context of the target users. As the system is designed not to be a dental practitioner system but instead a general user one, simplicity, accessibility, and also feedback clarity is emphasized. Meanwhile, the system should be technically sound to be able to handle dental photographs and offer any useful preventive data without drawing clinical conclusions.

In terms of functionality, the system has to enable users to take or post dental pictures with their smartphone cameras and send them to be analyzed. The system should be capable of processing such images with the help of a machine learning model and be able to detect visible signs of typical cases of oral health issues, including plaque accumulation, tooth discoloration, and gingival inflammation. The system is supposed to provide results after analysis in clear and understandable format, which should offer preventive advice and motivate people to consult a dentist on cases where they need professional dental treatment. These functions respond to the stated gap in the realization of oral health awareness and screening outside of clinical practice.

Besides basic functionality, the system should have basic user management and data management. This involves safe storage of uploaded photos and analysis findings, reference and follow up of results by linking with individual users. Since health related data is sensitive, the system must have some fundamental data protection provisions such as limited access and secure data storage, which are part of ethical standards of digital health systems.

The non-functional requirements also play a significant role in making the SmartSmile system effective. The system should be user-friendly by people who have no technical and medical knowledge and should have a user-friendly interface and low user input. Performance requirements comprise of reasonable response time to image analysis to aid the real time feedback, even when the network is limited. The reliability and scalability are also taken into consideration wherein the system must not experience any performance hitches when it accommodates more than one user.

On the whole, the system requirements analysis will provide clear boundaries on what will be done by the SmartSmile system and what will not be done by the system. This stage offers a solid basis to the future system design and implementation phases by concentrating on preventive screening instead of diagnosis, and ensuring that the technical capabilities are matched with the user needs and ethical issues.

**3.3 	Class Diagram**

This is a class diagram which shows the key structural elements of the SmartSmile system and its interactions. The User type is the type of people who will be involved in the application as they capture and upload dental pictures. The Image Analysis Service analyzes every Dental Image with machine learning to detect visible oral health indicators. An Analysis Result is generated by the analysis that includes the result like plaque, caries or gingivitis. According to these findings, the Preventive Feedback class is able to produce risk information and user-friendly advice to promote early awareness and professional consultation. The preventive goal of the SmartSmile can be achieved with this design of the classes as the interaction between users, analysis of the image, and generation of feedback are organized clearly and can be scaled.  
**![][image1]**

**3.4 System Architecture** 

**![][image2]**  
SmartSmile system is based on a client-server model where the user captures the dental images using a smartphone and sends the images to a back-end server to process the images. The backend manages image processing, interacts with the machine learning model to analyze images, and puts all the appropriate information in the database. The system then produces preventive feedback and sent back to the web interface to be displayed by the user.   
**3.5 UML Diagrams**  
**Entity Relationship Diagram,**   
This data structure of the SmartSmile system and the interaction of its main entities are presented in this Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD). The User entity will depict the people who utilise the system and may create a number of Dental Image records, which is a one-to-many relationship. All Dental images are processed to generate one Analysis Result, which records the oral health indicators, including plaque, caries and gingival inflammation. According to the result of the analysis, the system produces the Preventive Feedback as detailing the level of risk and offering the individual oral health recommendations to the user. This ERD guarantees proper structure of data, facilitates the traceability of receiving an image to a feedback and the preventive nature of the SmartSmile system without clinical diagnosis.  
![][image3]

**3.6 Development Tools**

The SmartSmile project is suggested to use a combination of software development and research tools, which are appropriate to develop a smartphone-accessible preventive oral health screening system. The tools are chosen according to their applicability to machine learning, development of mobile accessible web system, and the design of the system, but also based on their availability to academic research.

In terms of **machine learning and image analysis** component, Python is recommended as the main programming language because it has a wide range of support to artificial intelligence and computer vision research. The convolutional neural network (CNN) models will train and be developed on machine learning libraries like TensorFlow and Keras and will be used to analyze the dental images captured by smartphones. It suggests that OpenCV can be used to perform image preprocessing operation, such as resizing, normalization, and quality improvement to enhance the model performance under non-clinical conditions of image capturing.

The **backend platform** will be built with the Flask, a lightweight python web framework, which will help in communicating between the mobile application and the machine learning model or Firebase. The backend will receive the duties of receiving the image uploads, analysis request and delivering the preventive screening outcome to the user in real time.

In the case of the **web based interface**, responsive design will be proposed to offer a simple and easy-to-use interface. This interface will allow the user to take or upload the dental images via his smartphone cameras or other devices, transmit the images to be analyzed, and give feedback on preventive oral care. It is desirable to have a web-based solution as it is available on various devices and operating systems without installation, and this contributes to scalability and ease of deployment without affecting the efficiency of development.

Within **systems design and documentation**, draw.io and Microsoft Word/Google Doc are suggested to be utilized in modeling UML diagrams, entity relationship diagrams, and system architecture representations. These tools will help in visualization of system components and data association needed to analyse the system and to present academically.

To **test and evaluate**, the standard metrics of evaluation proposed include accuracy, precision, recall, and response time which can be used to evaluate the performance of the image analysis model and the system as well. Structured questionnaires will be used to gather information on the user feedback to analyze the usability, understandability of the feedback and perceived preventive value of the system.

###
